content.text: What It Is

Load testing LLM API paths means driving an agentic application with synthetic concurrent traffic to measure how the system behaves as the number of simultaneous users, requests, or active conversations grows. Unlike load testing a traditional REST API — where the core question is requests per second — LLM API load testing must account for an additional resource dimension: tokens. Every call to an LLM API consumes input and output tokens, and providers enforce both a requests-per-minute (RPM) limit and a tokens-per-minute (TPM) limit independently. A single user sending a 4,000-token prompt followed by a 2,000-token completion can exhaust a TPM budget far faster than ten users exchanging brief messages.
The tools most commonly used for this are
Locust
(a Python-based, scriptable load generator) and
k6

(a JavaScript/Go tool with first-class custom metrics support). Both simulate virtual users that follow a realistic scenario — authenticating, selecting a prompt, sending a chat completion request, receiving a streaming or batch response, and then measuring how long each stage took. The test runner scales from a handful of users on a laptop to hundreds of thousands of virtual users distributed across a cloud fleet.

In an agentic application, an LLM API path is not a single HTTP call. It is a multi-turn loop: the model responds, tools are invoked, tool results are appended, and the model responds again. Each iteration of that loop adds latency and consumes tokens. The tail latency and throughput ceiling of the agentic system as a whole therefore depends on how well the load test exercises the entire loop — not just a single completion endpoint.
Why It Matters

Production incidents at companies deploying LLM-powered features almost always have one of two root causes: rate limits were hit unexpectedly, or latency grew non-linearly under concurrency. Neither failure mode shows up in functional tests or unit tests, because those tests run sequentially with a single thread. The first time many teams discover that their agentic customer support system saturates its provider's TPM allocation at 50 simultaneous conversations — instead of the 500 they planned for — is during a launch event.

Load testing LLM API paths surfaces these problems before traffic is real. It answers questions that no other test category can: At what concurrency level do 429 rate-limit errors start appearing? How deep does the internal request queue grow before responses begin timing out? Does the first token in a streaming response arrive within 800 ms at 100 concurrent users but degrade to 3.5 seconds at 300? Is a multi-tool agent turn two to three times slower than a zero-tool turn, and does that gap widen under load?

The answers directly inform architectural decisions: whether to add a caching layer in front of the LLM, whether to implement a queue-and-worker pattern instead of synchronous passthrough, which tier of API access to purchase from the provider, and whether streaming is worth the added complexity of server-sent events in the client.
Benefits
Find the saturation point before production.
Load tests reveal the concurrency level at which 429 errors start and where p99 latency crosses SLA thresholds, giving teams a concrete number to design infrastructure around.
Validate retry and backoff logic under real pressure.
Exponential backoff code that looks correct in unit tests can have bugs that only manifest when multiple threads simultaneously hit a rate limit. A load test triggers those race conditions.
Measure realistic token throughput.
Tokens-per-second (TPS) is the correct throughput metric for LLM workloads. Load tests with representative prompt distributions yield a TPS figure that maps directly to provider tier selection.
Expose queue depth problems.
An agentic system that queues LLM requests internally will eventually stall if the queue grows faster than it drains. Load testing reveals the queue depth at which the system enters a degraded state.
Benchmark streaming vs. non-streaming tradeoffs.
Streaming provides faster time-to-first-token (TTFT) but adds server-side complexity. Load tests with both modes produce objective latency distributions to guide the choice.
Inform capacity planning and cost models.
The test run logs total tokens consumed, which feeds directly into monthly cost projections at projected user scale.
Trade-offs and Limitations

Load testing LLM APIs carries real monetary cost. Every virtual user call during a load test consumes tokens and is billed at standard rates. A 30-minute test with 100 concurrent users sending 500-token prompts and receiving 800-token responses can cost tens to hundreds of dollars depending on the model. Teams must budget for load testing explicitly and run large tests only when the system is close to production-ready.

Rate limits themselves complicate load testing. If the goal is to measure system behavior at high concurrency, but the provider rate-limits requests before the system under test is actually stressed, the test is measuring the provider's throttling behavior rather than the application's. Teams must negotiate higher rate-limit tiers with providers before running meaningful capacity tests, or use a staging environment with a mock LLM gateway that simulates rate-limit responses deterministically.
Non-deterministic response lengths.

LLM responses vary in length, making token consumption per request stochastic. Load test results have more variance than traditional API tests, requiring longer runs or more repeated experiments to produce stable percentile estimates.
Provider infrastructure variability.

LLM provider latency varies based on their own infrastructure load. A test run during provider peak hours will show higher latencies than one at off-peak times, making comparison across runs less reliable.
Streaming adds measurement complexity.
Measuring TTFT and streaming chunk latency requires instrumenting the HTTP response body byte-by-byte, which most off-the-shelf load testing tools do not support natively.
Prompt sensitivity.
The token budget and latency of a completion depend on the prompt. A test using unrealistically short prompts will underestimate real-world token consumption and latency.
How It Works
The fundamental unit of measurement in LLM load testing is the
virtual user (VU)

— a simulated client that repeats a scenario loop: pick a prompt, make an HTTP request to the LLM API path, receive the response (streaming or batch), record timing data, and optionally sleep before the next iteration. The load generator spawns hundreds or thousands of VUs simultaneously, each running independently.
Provider rate limits operate via a
token bucket model

. The provider maintains a bucket with a fixed capacity — for example, 90,000 tokens per minute. Each token consumed drains the bucket by that amount, and the bucket refills at a steady rate (1,500 tokens per second for a 90k TPM limit). When the bucket is empty, subsequent requests receive a 429 Too Many Requests response, usually with a
Retry-After
or
x-ratelimit-reset-tokens
header indicating when the bucket will have enough capacity for the next request. There are separate buckets for RPM and TPM, and a request must fit within both budgets simultaneously.
A well-designed load test measures several distinct phases: a
ramp-up phase
where VUs are added gradually to identify the concurrency threshold at which latency begins to increase; a
sustained load phase
where VU count is held constant to measure steady-state throughput and latency percentiles; a
spike phase
that adds a sudden burst of users to test recovery when the token bucket drains abruptly; and a
soak phase
that runs sustained load for hours to detect memory leaks in the queue manager or gradual degradation in retry logic.

For streaming endpoints, time-to-first-token (TTFT) is the most user-visible metric. It is measured as the elapsed time from sending the HTTP request to receiving the first server-sent-event (SSE) data chunk with a model token. Subsequent chunk latency — the gap between consecutive SSE events — determines how smooth the text appears to stream. Both are measured inside the VU's response handler by recording timestamps at each SSE boundary.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Load testing architecture for LLM API paths

content.text: The load generator runs virtual users that send HTTP requests to the LLM Gateway — the application layer that enforces rate limits, manages queues, routes to the correct model endpoint, and handles retries. The gateway forwards to two downstream targets: the LLM API (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, or a self-hosted model) for completions, and external Tool APIs for search, database, or internal service calls. Metrics from all three layers flow to a metrics collector and then to an observability dashboard. The dashboard surfaces the four panels that matter most: throughput (tokens/sec and requests/min), latency (TTFT, p50, p95, p99), error rates (429s, timeouts, 5xx), and queue depth (pending and in-flight requests).
When to Use It

Run LLM API load tests before launching a feature to production users. The minimum useful test checks whether the system handles the expected peak concurrent-conversation count without degrading to unacceptable latency. A reasonable minimum SLA for an interactive agentic feature is TTFT under 1.5 seconds at p95 with zero 429 errors at target concurrency.
Run when scaling up user counts.
Non-linear behaviors — queue saturation, connection pool exhaustion — may not manifest until specific thresholds are crossed. Any time expected peak concurrent users doubles, re-run the load test.
Run after changing prompt templates.

Longer prompts consume more input tokens per request. A template change that adds 500 tokens of context increases TPM consumption proportionally and can push a system that previously ran comfortably under rate limits to begin hitting them.
Run when switching models.
Different models have different latency profiles and token limits per tier. Switching models without re-testing can invalidate SLA assumptions made against the previous model.
Do not run against production without a spending limit.
Uncontrolled load tests can consume significant token budgets in minutes. Always configure provider spending limits or use a dedicated load-testing API key with a tight quota.
Do not use load tests to validate functional correctness.
Load tests measure performance characteristics, not answer quality. Functional and evaluation testing require separate test runs with graded outputs.
How to Implement

A practical implementation follows five steps. First, define the load profile: the target concurrent-user count, the ramp duration, the sustained duration, and whether a spike or soak phase is needed. Second, build a representative prompt corpus — a collection of prompts that reflects the real distribution the system will receive in production, including the range of input lengths, the fraction of requests that trigger tool calls, and the fraction of multi-turn vs. single-turn conversations. A corpus of 50–200 prompts is usually sufficient for stable averages.
Third, instrument the VU scenario to capture the metrics that matter: wall-clock request duration, TTFT (for streaming), total tokens consumed (read from the response body or from
x-ratelimit-remaining-tokens

headers), and the HTTP status code. Fourth, define success thresholds — for example, p95 response time below 3 seconds and the 429 error rate below 0.5% — and configure the test runner to fail the run if thresholds are breached. Fifth, collect provider-side metrics by polling the
x-ratelimit-remaining-tokens
and
x-ratelimit-remaining-requests
response headers on each call and emitting them as custom metrics to the dashboard.
Install the load generator:
pip install locust
or download the k6 binary.
Build a prompt corpus file (CSV or JSON) with at least 30 representative prompts covering short, medium, and long token lengths.
Write the VU scenario script, handling 429 responses with exponential backoff inside the scenario rather than relying on the load generator's default error counting.
Run a short smoke test (5 VUs, 2 minutes) to validate the script before committing to a full run.
Run the full load profile and export results to a persistent store (InfluxDB, CSV, k6 Cloud, or Locust's built-in CSV export).
Compare results against the defined SLA thresholds and file tickets for any regressions before the next deployment.
Examples
Locust: Basic LLM API Load Test

The following Locust locustfile simulates users sending chat completion requests to an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, measures response times, handles 429 rate-limit errors with exponential backoff, and tracks total tokens consumed as a custom counter.

# locustfile.py — LLM API load test with Locust
# pip install locust
# Run: locust -f locustfile.py --users 50 --spawn-rate 5 --run-time 5m --headless --csv results

import random
import time
from locust import HttpUser, task, between, events
import gevent

PROMPT_CORPUS = [
    "Summarize the key differences between supervised and unsupervised learning in two sentences.",
    "Write a Python function that computes the nth Fibonacci number iteratively.",
    "What are the main causes of the 2008 financial crisis? Provide a brief overview.",
    "Explain the concept of attention in transformer models at a high level.",
    "Draft a short professional email declining a meeting due to a scheduling conflict.",
    "List three advantages of containerization in software deployment.",
    "What is the difference between TCP and UDP? When would you use each?",
    "Write SQL to find the top 5 customers by total order value from an orders table.",
    "Explain how gradient descent works in one paragraph.",
    "Describe the CAP theorem and what it means for distributed systems.",
]

total_tokens_consumed = 0


class LLMAPIUser(HttpUser):
    wait_time = between(1, 3)
    host = "https://api.openai.com"

    def on_start(self):
        import os
        self.api_key = os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"]
        self.headers = {
            "Authorization": f"Bearer {self.api_key}",
            "Content-Type": "application/json",
        }
        self.max_retries = 4

    @task
    def chat_completion(self):
        prompt = random.choice(PROMPT_CORPUS)
        payload = {
            "model": "gpt-4o-mini",
            "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
            "max_tokens": 512,
            "stream": False,
        }
        for attempt in range(self.max_retries):
            with self.client.post(
                "/v1/chat/completions",
                json=payload,
                headers=self.headers,
                name="/v1/chat/completions",
                catch_response=True,
            ) as response:
                if response.status_code == 200:
                    tokens = response.json().get("usage", {}).get("total_tokens", 0)
                    global total_tokens_consumed
                    total_tokens_consumed += tokens
                    response.success()
                    return
                elif response.status_code == 429:
                    wait = (2 ** attempt) + random.uniform(0, 1)
                    response.failure(f"429 rate limited (attempt {attempt + 1})")
                    if attempt < self.max_retries - 1:
                        gevent.sleep(wait)
                else:
                    response.failure(f"HTTP {response.status_code}")
                    return


@events.quitting.add_listener
def on_quitting(environment, **kwargs):
    print(f"\nTotal tokens consumed during test: {total_tokens_consumed:,}")
k6: LLM Load Test with Custom Metrics and Threshold Gates

k6 uses JavaScript for scenario scripts and has first-class support for custom metrics. The following script tracks TTFT and emits tokens as a counter, with threshold gates that fail the CI job if SLAs are breached.
Measuring TTFT for Streaming Endpoints
Streaming responses require byte-level instrumentation. The following Python example measures TTFT by detecting the first non-empty SSE data chunk in a streaming completion response.
Queue Depth Testing Under Burst Load

Agentic systems often queue LLM requests internally to prevent overwhelming the provider. The following example tests queue behavior by submitting bursts of concurrent requests and monitoring queue depth through a health endpoint exposed by the gateway.

# queue_depth_test.py — burst load test monitoring internal queue depth
# Assumes GET /health/queue returns {"pending": N, "in_flight": N}
import asyncio, aiohttp, time
from statistics import quantiles

SERVICE_URL = "http://localhost:8080"
CONCURRENCY = 80
TOTAL_REQUESTS = 400

async def send_request(session, prompt):
    start = time.monotonic()
    try:
        async with session.post(
            f"{SERVICE_URL}/api/chat",
            json={"message": prompt},
            timeout=aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=60),
        ) as resp:
            await resp.read()
            return {"status": resp.status, "ms": (time.monotonic() - start) * 1000}
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        return {"status": 504, "ms": 60_000}

async def poll_queue(session, interval, readings):
    while True:
        try:
            async with session.get(f"{SERVICE_URL}/health/queue") as r:
                if r.status == 200:
                    readings.append(await r.json())
        except Exception:
            pass
        await asyncio.sleep(interval)

async def run_test():
    prompts = ["Summarize the water cycle.", "Write a haiku about recursion.",
               "List the OSI model layers."] * (TOTAL_REQUESTS // 3 + 1)
    queue_readings, results = [], []
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        poller = asyncio.create_task(poll_queue(session, 0.5, queue_readings))
        sem = asyncio.Semaphore(CONCURRENCY)
        async def bounded(p):
            async with sem: return await send_request(session, p)
        results = await asyncio.gather(*[bounded(p) for p in prompts[:TOTAL_REQUESTS]])
        poller.cancel()

    ok = [r["ms"] for r in results if r["status"] == 200]
    if ok:
        p = quantiles(ok, n=100)
        print(f"p50={p[49]:.0f}ms  p95={p[94]:.0f}ms  p99={p[98]:.0f}ms")
    print(f"Errors: {len(results) - len(ok)}")
    if queue_readings:
        print(f"Peak pending: {max(r.get('pending', 0) for r in queue_readings)}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(run_test())
Full-Jitter Exponential Backoff

The following utility implements the full-jitter backoff pattern recommended for LLM API rate limits. It reads the provider-supplied retry delay from response headers when available and falls back to computed backoff otherwise.

# backoff.py — full-jitter exponential backoff for LLM API retries
import time, random, httpx
from typing import Callable

def call_with_backoff(
    fn: Callable[[], httpx.Response],
    max_retries: int = 6,
    base_delay: float = 1.0,
    max_delay: float = 60.0,
) -> httpx.Response:
    """
    Retries fn() on HTTP 429 or 503 with full-jitter exponential backoff.
    Full jitter: wait = random.uniform(0, min(max_delay, base * 2**attempt))
    This avoids the thundering-herd problem where all clients retry
    simultaneously, immediately re-saturating the provider's token bucket.
    """
    for attempt in range(max_retries + 1):
        response = fn()
        if response.status_code not in (429, 503):
            return response
        if attempt == max_retries:
            return response

        # Respect provider-supplied Retry-After header if present
        provider_wait = 0.0
        retry_after = response.headers.get("retry-after")
        if retry_after:
            try:
                provider_wait = float(retry_after)
            except ValueError:
                pass

        cap = min(max_delay, base_delay * (2 ** attempt))
        wait = max(provider_wait, random.uniform(0, cap))
        print(f"Rate limited (attempt {attempt + 1}/{max_retries}). Waiting {wait:.2f}s")
        time.sleep(wait)

    return response  # unreachable; satisfies type checkers
Real-World Example

Consider an agentic customer support system deployed for an e-commerce platform. Each conversation is an agentic loop that may invoke a product database lookup tool, an order-status API tool, and a returns-policy retrieval tool before generating a final response. The product team expects up to 300 simultaneous conversations during peak shopping hours.

The team runs a Locust load test with a scenario mirroring real traffic: 40% of conversations are simple one-turn queries (average 200 input tokens, 150 output tokens), 45% require one tool call (average 400 input tokens across two LLM calls), and 15% are complex multi-tool conversations (average 800 input tokens across three calls). They build a corpus of 80 representative support queries weighted to match these fractions.

At 200 concurrent conversations, p95 response time is 3.8 seconds — within the 5-second SLA — but the team observes occasional 429 bursts lasting 10–15 seconds, caused by tool-call-heavy conversations exhausting the token budget in short windows. At 300 concurrent conversations, the 429 rate reaches 4% and the gateway queue grows to 80 pending requests, causing p99 latency to spike above 12 seconds.

The fix is a token-aware request scheduler in the gateway: before submitting an LLM call, the scheduler estimates the request's token count from the message history and checks the current remaining-tokens budget via the
x-ratelimit-remaining-tokens

response header. If the bucket is below a safety threshold, the request waits 200 ms and retries the check. After deploying the scheduler and re-running the load test, p99 at 300 concurrent conversations drops to 5.2 seconds with a 0.1% 429 rate.
Common Mistakes
Using Unrealistic Prompt Lengths

Load tests that use a single short prompt for every virtual user vastly underestimate token consumption and latency. A 10-token input prompt takes a fraction of the processing time of a 2,000-token context window. The rate-limit headroom visible in a short-prompt test disappears when real users send long conversation histories. Always build a prompt corpus that reflects the actual input length distribution, including tail cases with large context windows.
Ignoring the Token Dimension of Rate Limits

Teams that focus only on requests-per-minute are often surprised when they hit TPM limits well before the RPM limit. A system with a 60,000 TPM limit and a 500 RPM limit can hit the TPM ceiling with as few as 120 requests per minute if each request averages 500 tokens. Always track both RPM and TPM consumption during load tests and report both utilization percentages separately.
Not Distinguishing Handled vs. Escaped 429 Errors

A 429 during a load test is not necessarily a bug — it may be the correct behavior when the test intentionally exceeds the provider's rate limit. The critical distinction is between 429s that are handled by the backoff logic (expected, should be invisible to the user) and 429s that escape to the caller (a failure). Instrument the retry code to distinguish these categories and report them as separate metrics on the dashboard.
Running Load Tests Against Production Without Safeguards

An uncontrolled load test against a production API key can exhaust a monthly token budget in minutes, degrade service for real users, and generate a large unexpected bill. Always use a dedicated load-testing API key with a provider spending limit, and run tests against a staging environment that mirrors production architecture but is isolated from live traffic.
Best Practices
Build a representative prompt corpus.
Sample 50–200 prompts from production logs (anonymized). Weight their selection probabilities in the VU scenario to match the actual distribution of short, medium, and long inputs.
Track tokens per second as the primary throughput metric.
Requests-per-second is a secondary metric for LLM workloads. Total tokens consumed divided by test duration gives TPS, which maps directly to provider TPM limits and cost projections.
Use staged load profiles, not step functions.
Ramp VUs gradually (10 new VUs every 30 seconds) to identify the exact concurrency threshold at which behavior changes, rather than jumping directly to peak load.
Test streaming and non-streaming separately.
They exercise different code paths and have different TTFT characteristics. Maintain separate test scenarios for each mode and report their metrics independently.
Read rate-limit headers on every response.
The
x-ratelimit-remaining-tokens
and
x-ratelimit-remaining-requests
headers are available on every OpenAI and Anthropic response. Emit them as custom metrics to visualize token bucket utilization in real time.
Implement full-jitter backoff, not fixed backoff.
Fixed backoff causes all retrying VUs to wake up simultaneously and re-saturate the rate limit. Full jitter spreads retries randomly across the backoff window, reducing the thundering-herd effect.
Define and enforce SLA thresholds in the test runner.
Use k6 thresholds or Locust's failure ratio check to automatically fail the run if p95 latency or error rate exceeds defined limits, making load tests part of CI gates.
Save results to a time-series store.
Export Locust CSV or k6 JSON to InfluxDB so that load test results can be compared across releases and regressions identified over time.
Performance and Scalability

Throughput for an agentic LLM system scales along two independent axes: the application layer's concurrency capacity (connections, goroutines, async tasks) and the provider's rate-limit budget. Because these ceilings are independent, the system can hit either one first. A well-provisioned agentic gateway can handle hundreds of concurrent in-flight connections to the LLM API, but will be throttled well before that by a standard-tier TPM limit.

Token consumption accumulates non-linearly in multi-turn agents. A five-turn conversation does not consume five times the tokens of a one-turn conversation, because each subsequent turn appends the full prior context. Turn 1 might use 200 input tokens; turn 2 uses 200 + 150 (prior output) + 200 (new input) = 550 input tokens; turn 3 uses 550 + 400 + 200 = 1,150. The total grows roughly quadratically with the number of turns, making long-running agents disproportionately expensive under load.
Horizontal scaling of the gateway.

The application gateway scales horizontally, but the provider rate limit is per API key. Multiple gateway instances sharing one key do not multiply the token budget. To scale beyond a single key's limits, load-balance across multiple API keys or multiple provider accounts.
Context truncation as a throughput lever.

Truncating old turns in the conversation history before appending a new message caps input token growth per turn, trading some response quality for throughput headroom. Load tests should run with and without truncation to quantify the improvement.
Caching for repeated prompts.

If a significant fraction of incoming prompts are semantically similar, a semantic caching layer can serve cached responses without consuming provider tokens. Load tests on the cached vs. uncached path quantify the token savings.
Provider tier selection.

Load test results translate directly to tier requirements: if steady-state consumption is 45,000 TPM at 200 concurrent users, the team needs a tier with at least 60,000 TPM (adding a 33% headroom buffer).

Logic correctness only, not performance

A mock-gateway approach is invaluable for testing application-layer queuing and retry logic without paying provider costs. The gateway can be configured to return 429 responses at a controlled rate and with configurable latency, allowing backoff logic to be exercised under realistic conditions at zero token cost. Once the application layer is validated against the mock gateway, a smaller live-API test confirms that end-to-end latency at the real provider matches expectations.
Security and Reliability
Load test scripts almost always contain API keys. These must never be committed to source control. Use environment variables (
OPENAI_API_KEY
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY

) or a secrets manager. The key used for load testing should be a dedicated key distinct from the production key, with its own spending limit set at the provider dashboard to prevent runaway costs if the script is misconfigured.

Rate-limit responses (429) during a load test are information, not errors to suppress. Log every 429 with its timestamp, the remaining-tokens header value, and the retry delay used. A 429 that triggers correct backoff and eventually succeeds is a healthy signal; a 429 that causes the VU to immediately retry and generate a cascade of 429s is a bug in the retry logic that must be fixed before the system goes to production.
Set provider spending limits before any load test.
A misconfigured concurrency setting can generate thousands of dollars of token charges in minutes. Most providers allow per-key monthly spending limits.
Isolate load test environments.
Use a staging deployment network-isolated from production. Synthetic traffic must not share a database, cache, or queue with real user traffic.
Test circuit breakers explicitly.

If the system uses a circuit breaker to shed LLM load when the error rate exceeds a threshold, the load test must exercise the condition that trips the circuit and verify that the system gracefully degrades rather than throwing unhandled exceptions.
Verify graceful shutdown under load.
Stop the load test abruptly and verify that the gateway drains its queue cleanly, in-flight requests complete or time out gracefully, and no orphaned requests are left open against the provider.
Use synthetic prompts, not sampled user conversations.
Load test prompts should be purpose-built, not sampled from real user conversations, to avoid logging personally identifiable information or confidential business content in test infrastructure logs.
Key Takeaways
LLM API load testing must measure two resource dimensions — requests per minute (RPM) and tokens per minute (TPM) — because either can be the binding constraint at different concurrency levels.

Locust and k6 are the primary tools; Locust excels for Python-native scenarios with direct SDK integration, while k6 is better suited for CI-gated threshold enforcement and cloud-distributed execution.

Streaming endpoints require custom byte-level instrumentation to measure time-to-first-token (TTFT); standard load testing request-duration metrics capture total response time, not the user-visible first-token delay.

The token bucket model governs provider rate limiting: requests are throttled when the bucket drains, not when request count alone hits a ceiling, making prompt length distribution the most important variable in load test design.

Tool-calling turns in agentic applications consume roughly twice the tokens and take twice as long as zero-tool turns; load test scenarios must include the tool-call path weighted by its real-world occurrence rate.

Full-jitter exponential backoff prevents the thundering-herd problem when multiple VUs are rate-limited simultaneously; fixed or linear backoff causes synchronized retry storms that immediately re-saturate the token bucket.
Use a dedicated API key with a provider spending limit for load tests; run against a staging environment isolated from production traffic; never commit API keys to test scripts.

Load test results map directly to architecture decisions: token-aware request schedulers, semantic caches, context truncation policies, and provider tier selection all derive their justification from measured saturation points and throughput curves.
